aliases.description: Nickname for the dark albedo feature Solis Lacus, which sits in a brighter region like the pupil of an eye; noted for variability by early observers.
Popular nickname for Valles Marineris, the ~4000 km canyon system dwarfing Earth's Grand Canyon.
Nickname for Galle crater, whose interior ridges and dune fields resemble a smiley face in orbital images.
Rectilinear ridge network near the south pole resembling ruined walls; officially named Angustus Labyrinthus, likely dune casts or clastic dikes.
Informal NASA name for Aeolis Mons honoring geologist Robert P. Sharp; the ~5.5 km high layered central mound of Gale crater that Curiosity has been climbing since 2014.

colloquial.name: Adirondack
colloquial.description: Spirit's first rock target and first use of the Rock Abrasion Tool; olivine basalt.

colloquial.name: Airy-0
colloquial.description: Small crater inside Airy chosen to define the martian prime meridian (0 deg longitude); the name is a cartographic convention, not an IAU feature name.

colloquial.name: Bagnold Dunes
colloquial.description: First active dune field studied in situ on another planet, on the northwest flank of Mount Sharp; named for dune scientist Ralph Bagnold.

colloquial.name: Barnacle Bill
colloquial.description: First rock analyzed by the Sojourner rover's APXS; surprisingly andesite-like composition.

colloquial.name: Big Crater
colloquial.description: Kilometer-scale crater whose rim was visible on the horizon about 2 km from the Mars Pathfinder landing site.

colloquial.name: Big Joe
colloquial.description: Basaltic boulder about 2 m across near the Viking 1 lander, one of the first individually named rocks on Mars.

colloquial.name: Block Island
colloquial.description: Iron-nickel meteorite about 0.67 m across, the largest yet found on Mars.

colloquial.name: Bonneville crater
colloquial.description: First crater target of the Spirit rover; named for the ancient Lake Bonneville of Utah. Spirit imaged its own heat shield on the far rim.

colloquial.name: Bounce Rock
colloquial.description: Rock struck by Opportunity's airbags on landing; composition closely matches basaltic shergottite meteorites found on Earth.

colloquial.name: Bright Angel
colloquial.description: Light-toned outcrop area in an ancient river channel (Grand Canyon naming theme); hosts the Cheyava Falls rock.

colloquial.name: Burns Cliff
colloquial.description: Cross-bedded sulfate-rich cliff in Endurance crater; named for MIT mineralogist Roger Burns, who predicted such mineralogy.

colloquial.name: Cape York
colloquial.description: Rim segment of Endeavour where Opportunity found the gypsum vein 'Homestake', clear evidence of water flowing through fractures.

colloquial.name: Cheyava Falls
colloquial.region: Bright Angel, Neretva Vallis, Jezero crater
colloquial.description: Arrowhead-shaped, vein-filled rock with 'leopard spot' reaction fronts - a potential biosignature; cored as sample 'Sapphire Canyon'.

colloquial.name: Columbia Hills
colloquial.description: Range of seven hills near Spirit's landing site, named in memory of the STS-107 Space Shuttle Columbia crew.

colloquial.name: Comanche
colloquial.description: Carbonate-rich outcrop found by Spirit - evidence of near-neutral, carbon-dioxide-charged ancient waters.

colloquial.name: Dodo-Goldilocks
colloquial.description: Robotic-arm trench in which bright chunks sublimated away over four days, confirming shallow subsurface water ice.

colloquial.name: Eagle crater
colloquial.description: Small crater in which Opportunity rolled to a stop (a 'hole in one'); named for the Apollo 11 lunar module and the golf term. Contains the El Capitan outcrop.

colloquial.name: Egg Rock
colloquial.description: Golf-ball-size iron-nickel meteorite analyzed by Curiosity's ChemCam laser.

colloquial.name: El Capitan
colloquial.description: Finely layered outcrop with sulfates and hematite 'blueberries'; provided the first strong in-situ evidence of standing liquid water on ancient Mars.

colloquial.name: El Dorado
colloquial.description: Dark sand dune field on the southern flank of Husband Hill sampled by Spirit.

colloquial.name: Endurance crater
colloquial.description: Crater explored by Opportunity in 2004; named for Ernest Shackleton's ship Endurance. Site of Burns Cliff.

colloquial.name: Erebus crater
colloquial.description: Shallow, eroded crater visited by Opportunity between Endurance and Victoria; named for the polar exploration ship HMS Erebus.

colloquial.name: Face on Mars
colloquial.description: Mesa that resembled a humanoid face in a low-resolution 1976 Viking 1 image; later high-resolution imaging showed an ordinary eroded mesa.

colloquial.name: Glen Torridon
colloquial.description: Clay-bearing valley between Vera Rubin Ridge and the Greenheugh Pediment, rich in smectites; named for a Scottish glen.

colloquial.name: Glenelg
colloquial.description: Junction of three terrain types east of Bradbury Landing; the palindromic name was chosen because the rover would pass through it twice.

colloquial.name: Greenheugh Pediment
colloquial.description: Sandstone-capped sloping surface on Mount Sharp; Curiosity's steepest climb to that date crossed its edge.

colloquial.name: Heat Shield Rock
colloquial.description: Iron-nickel meteorite found beside Opportunity's discarded heat shield; the first meteorite identified on another planet (official meteorite name: Meridiani Planum).

colloquial.name: Hogwallow Flats
colloquial.description: Very fine-grained, light-toned 'bacon strip' outcrop area on the delta, promising for organic preservation.

colloquial.name: Home Plate
colloquial.description: Roughly home-plate-shaped plateau of layered volcaniclastic rock near the Columbia Hills; evidence of explosive volcanism and hydrothermal silica nearby.

colloquial.name: Homestead hollow
colloquial.description: Degraded, sand-filled impact crater in which the InSight lander came to rest; its smooth fill aided seismometer placement.

colloquial.name: Husband Hill
colloquial.description: Tallest of the Columbia Hills, named for Columbia commander Rick Husband; summited by Spirit in 2005 - the first summit climbed on another planet.

colloquial.name: Jake Matijevic
colloquial.description: Pyramidal rock, Curiosity's first contact-science target; a mugearite unlike previously analyzed martian rocks; named for a rover engineer.

colloquial.name: John Klein
colloquial.description: Site of the first drill hole ever made on Mars; the mudstone showed an ancient habitable lake environment; named for an MSL deputy project manager.

colloquial.name: Kodiak
colloquial.description: Isolated butte preserving inclined delta strata; its layering gave key confirmation that Jezero once held a lake with a river delta.

colloquial.name: Lookout Hill
colloquial.description: Point at which Perseverance crested the western rim of Jezero crater in December 2024 after its climb out of the crater interior.

colloquial.name: Marathon Valley
colloquial.description: Valley cutting Endeavour's rim with clay-mineral signatures; named as Opportunity's odometry surpassed a marathon distance (42.2 km).

colloquial.name: Marker Band Valley
colloquial.description: Valley containing a thin, dark, resistant 'marker band'; Curiosity found wave-ripple textures there - evidence of an ancient shallow lake.

colloquial.name: McCool Hill
colloquial.description: Hill named for Columbia pilot William McCool; a planned Spirit wintering target.

colloquial.name: Mont Mercou
colloquial.description: Roughly 6 m high cliff studied and drilled by Curiosity ('Nontron' drill hole); named for a hill in France, honoring the French ChemCam partnership.

colloquial.name: Murray Buttes
colloquial.description: Group of buttes and mesas of eroded sandstone at the gateway to upper Mount Sharp; named for planetary scientist Bruce Murray.

colloquial.name: Máaz
colloquial.description: Perseverance's first rock target, basaltic crater-floor material; the name is the Navajo word for Mars.

colloquial.name: Namib Dune
colloquial.description: Barchan dune with a ~4 m slip face; subject of a famous Curiosity self-portrait and sand sampling.

colloquial.name: Pahrump Hills
colloquial.description: First outcrop of the lake-deposited Murray formation at the base of Mount Sharp; site of a walkabout survey and several drill holes.

colloquial.name: Perseverance Valley
colloquial.description: Shallow trough possibly carved by fluids or debris flows; Opportunity was descending it when the 2018 global dust storm ended the mission.

colloquial.name: Pot of Gold
colloquial.description: Oddly resistant nodular rock containing hematite, found at the base of the Columbia Hills.

colloquial.name: Purgatory Dune
colloquial.description: Wind-blown ripple in which Opportunity's wheels dug in, trapping the rover for about five weeks.

colloquial.name: Rafael Navarro Mountain
colloquial.description: Butte on Mount Sharp named in memory of astrobiologist Rafael Navarro-Gonzalez of the Curiosity science team.

colloquial.name: Rochette
colloquial.description: Briefcase-size rock from which Perseverance extracted its first successfully cached core samples ('Montdenier' and 'Montagnac').

colloquial.name: Rocknest
colloquial.description: Sand drift where Curiosity took its first scooped soil samples and first self-portrait.

colloquial.name: Rolling Stones Rock
colloquial.description: Pebble rolled about a meter by InSight's landing thrusters; named for the rock band The Rolling Stones.

colloquial.name: Santa Maria crater
colloquial.description: Fresh crater with hydrated sulfate signatures on its rim, studied by Opportunity en route to Endeavour; named for Columbus's flagship.

colloquial.name: Scooby-Doo
colloquial.description: Flat whitish rock near Pathfinder, interpreted as cemented soil or evaporite-like material.

colloquial.name: Skinner Ridge
colloquial.description: Sedimentary outcrop containing clasts transported from outside Jezero; cored for sample return.

colloquial.name: Sleepy Hollow
colloquial.description: Shallow, dust-filled hollow near Spirit's landing site, an early observation target.

colloquial.name: Snow Queen
colloquial.description: Hard, smooth water-ice surface exposed beneath the Phoenix lander by its descent thrusters; first nicknamed 'Holy Cow!'.

colloquial.name: Solander Point
colloquial.description: North-tilted rim segment of Endeavour where Opportunity spent a winter working on clay-bearing rocks.

colloquial.name: Spiders (araneiform terrain)
colloquial.description: Radially branching channel systems carved by springtime carbon-dioxide gas jets beneath translucent seasonal ice.

colloquial.name: Swiss cheese terrain
colloquial.description: Flat-floored, rounded pits in the residual carbon-dioxide ice cap, resembling holes in Swiss cheese; they grow measurably each year.

colloquial.name: Séítah
colloquial.description: Dune-mantled unit of layered, olivine-rich rocks on Jezero's floor; Navajo for 'amidst the sand'; scouted from the air by the Ingenuity helicopter.

colloquial.name: The Kimberley
colloquial.description: Science waypoint of interbedded sandstones named for the Australian region; drill target 'Windjana'.

colloquial.name: Three Forks
colloquial.description: Flat area at the delta's base where Perseverance built the first sample depot on another planet - ten backup tubes dropped in 2022-2023.

colloquial.name: Troy
colloquial.description: Patch of soft sulfate-rich soil beside Home Plate in which Spirit became permanently embedded in 2009.

colloquial.name: Twin Peaks
colloquial.description: Pair of modest hills about 1 km southwest of the Pathfinder lander, prominent in panoramas of the landing site.

colloquial.name: Vera Rubin Ridge
colloquial.description: Hematite-bearing ridge on lower Mount Sharp traversed by Curiosity; named for astronomer Vera Rubin.

colloquial.name: Wildcat Ridge
colloquial.description: Mudstone outcrop with some of the highest organic-matter signals detected by Perseverance; cored for sample return.

colloquial.name: Witch Hazel Hill
colloquial.description: Layered outcrop sequence on Jezero's rim examined by Perseverance in 2025, exposing some of the oldest rocks visited by the mission.

colloquial.name: Yellowknife Bay
colloquial.description: Shallow topographic basin of ancient lakebed mudstone; Curiosity's first drill sites showed a past habitable environment.

colloquial.name: Yogi
colloquial.description: Large rounded basaltic rock, Sojourner's second analysis target; named for Yogi Bear.

landing_sites.name: Mars 2
landing_sites.notes: Descent system malfunctioned after entry at too steep an angle; first human-made object to reach the martian surface. Coordinates approximate.

landing_sites.name: Mars 3
landing_sites.notes: First soft landing on Mars; transmitted about 20 seconds of data (a featureless partial image) before contact was lost, likely due to a global dust storm. Coordinates approximate.

landing_sites.name: Mars 6
landing_sites.notes: Returned the first in-situ atmospheric profile data during descent; transmissions ceased at or just before landing. Coordinates approximate.

landing_sites.name: Viking 1
landing_sites.notes: First fully successful Mars landing; operated until November 1982. Ran the first life-detection experiments on Mars.

landing_sites.name: Viking 2
landing_sites.notes: Operated until April 1980; observed water frost on the surface.

landing_sites.name: Pathfinder
landing_sites.notes: First airbag landing and first rover (Sojourner) on Mars; operated about 3 months.

landing_sites.name: Mars Polar Lander
landing_sites.region: Ultimi Scopuli, south polar layered terrain (approx.)
landing_sites.notes: Contact lost during descent; premature engine shutdown (spurious touchdown signal) is the presumed cause. Coordinates approximate.

landing_sites.name: Deep Space 2
landing_sites.notes: Two impactor microprobes carried by Mars Polar Lander; never heard from after atmospheric entry. Coordinates approximate.

landing_sites.name: Spirit
landing_sites.notes: Operated over 6 years (planned 90 days); climbed Husband Hill; became embedded at 'Troy' in 2009; last contact March 2010.

landing_sites.name: Opportunity
landing_sites.notes: Drove a record 45.16 km over 14+ years; mission ended after the June 2018 global dust storm.

landing_sites.name: Phoenix
landing_sites.notes: First landing in the martian arctic; confirmed shallow subsurface water ice and detected perchlorates; operated ~5 months.

landing_sites.name: Curiosity
landing_sites.notes: First sky-crane landing; found ancient habitable lake environments; still operating (climbing Mount Sharp).

landing_sites.name: InSight
landing_sites.notes: First geophysics station on Mars; detected over 1300 marsquakes; mission ended December 2022 as dust reduced solar power.

landing_sites.name: Perseverance
landing_sites.notes: Caching samples for Mars Sample Return; Ingenuity made 72 flights (first powered flight on another planet, 2021-2024); Perseverance still operating on the crater rim.

landing_sites.name: Beagle 2
landing_sites.notes: Landed successfully but two solar panels failed to deploy, blocking the antenna; located intact in MRO images in 2015.

landing_sites.name: Schiaparelli
landing_sites.notes: Entry-descent demonstrator; a sensor fault caused premature thruster shutdown and free fall from ~3.7 km.

landing_sites.name: Zhurong
landing_sites.notes: Landed 2021-05-14 23:18 UTC = 2021-05-15 07:18 Beijing time (Chinese sources cite May 15). First non-US fully successful Mars landing; Zhurong drove ~1.9 km before entering hibernation in May 2022, from which it did not wake.
